claim_text: We have set ambitious, achievable metrics on carbon-emissions reductions.

Our metrics, coupled with our view of Scope 3—which includes supporting a price on carbon through well-designed policies; transparently reporting emissions from use of our products for nearly two decades; and enabling customers to lower their emissions through increasing our renewable products, offering offsets, and investing in low-carbon technologies—support a global approach in order to achieve the goals of the Paris Agreement as efficiently and cost-effectively as possible.
We believe achieving 24kg CO2e/boe for oil and for natural gas would place Chevron in the top quartile of total production, meaning among the most carbon-efficient producers.
This is our objective for 2028.
$750M by 2028 in investments in renewables and offsets.
$2B by 2028 in carbon-reduction projects.
$300M committed to the Future Energy Fund II.
upstream production net greenhouse gas emissions intensity reduction metrics for 2028: 24 kg CO2e/boe for oil (global industry averages 46) 40% reduction from 2016.
24 kg CO2e/boe for gas (global industry averages 71) 26% reduction from 2016.
2 kg CO2e/boe for methane and a global methane detection campaign 53% reduction from 2016.
0 routine flaring by 2030 and 3 kg CO2e/boe for overall flaring 66% reduction from 2016.

In 2018, Chevron established the Chevron Future Energy Fund with a commitment of $100 million, and a follow-up Future Energy Fund II in 2021 with a commitment of $300 million, to invest in breakthrough technologies that could enable the energy transition.

In 2020, more than 60 percent of our total Scope 1 and Scope 2 equity GHG emissions were in regions with existing or developing carbon-pricing policies, in addition to other lower-carbon policies like mandates for biofuels and renewables, methane regulation, and emerging support for technologies like carbon capture, utilization, and storage (CCUS) and hydrogen.

Chevron’s energy-transition strategy is to advance a lower-carbon future and we will leverage our capabilities, assets, and expertise to focus on three action areas that aim to deliver measurable progress that is both good for investors and good for society: Lowering carbon intensity cost-efficiently, Increasing renewables and offsets in support of our business, Investing in low-carbon technologies to enable commercial solutions.
According to the IPCC, achieving the Paris Agreement’s goals will require peaking emissions as soon as possible and global net-zero emissions by around 2070.
The IPCC finds that achieving a 1.5° C scenario with high confidence and without any temporary overshoot would require net zero by around 2050.
To achieve net-zero emissions by 2050, direct air carbon dioxide capture and storage and carbon capture and storage (CCS) are required to be scaled up and globally deployed.
Carbon-pricing mechanisms are in place or under development in 46 national and 35 subnational jurisdictions around the world
Our joint-venture Upstream assets are subject to the economywide carbon price of $22/tonne (CAD30).
A price on carbon has been in effect in Alberta since 2007.
A performance benchmark for large emitters was established under the Technology Innovation and Emissions Reduction program in 2020.
Our Upstream facilities are regulated by the federal Safeguard Mechanism that took effect in 2016.
Our Upstream interests are subject to the economywide carbon tax of $26/tonne (CAD35) for combustion emissions in effect since 2008.
The government implemented a carbon tax of $15/tonne (CAD20) in 2019 that increases to $37/tonne (CAD50) in 2022.
Our fuel supplies, along with others sold in the country, are subject to a $5/tonne (COP19,500) carbon tax in effect since 2017.
A carbon tax of $4/tonne (SGD5), in effect since 2019, is being applied to our joint-venture refinery and Oronite additive facility.
Newfoundland has agreed to work with the federal government to develop regulations, including limiting use of pneumatic devices, to reduce methane emissions by 45 percent by 2025.

A renewable-fuel-blending mandate in the state of New South Wales, in effect since 2007, and in the state of Queensland, in effect since 2017, applies to all fuel suppliers and requires that volumes of biofuel be blended into diesel and gasoline fuels.
A low-carbon-fuel mandate, in effect since 2011, applies to all fuel suppliers in California and sets carbon-intensity standards for gasoline, diesel, and the fuels that replace them.
A renewable-fuel-blending mandate, in effect since 2001, applies to all fuel suppliers and requires that volumes of biofuels, if available domestically, be blended into motor fuels.
Chevron’s Upstream operations are subject to methane rules that require leak detection and repair, and storage tank and other equipment controls.
A renewable-fuel-blending mandate, in effect since 2014, applies to all fuel suppliers and requires that volumes of biofuel be blended into diesel fuel.
A renewable-fuel-blending mandate, in effect since 2009, did apply to all fuel suppliers and required that volumes of biofuels be blended into gasoline and diesel fuels.
A renewable-fuel-blending mandate, in effect since 2007, applies to all fuel suppliers and requires that volumes of biofuels be blended into gasoline and diesel fuels.
A renewable-fuel-blending mandate, in effect since 2012, applies to all fuel suppliers and requires that volumes of biodiesel be blended into diesel fuel.
A renewable-fuel-blending mandate, in effect since 2006, requires the introduction of increasing volumes of biofuels into the U.S. fuel supply.
A renewable-fuel-blending mandate, in effect since 2008, applies to all fuel suppliers and requires that volumes of biofuels be blended into gasoline and diesel fuels.
State-level renewable-power targets: 8.5–60%
approximately 20 billion gallons were required in 2020
45Q tax credit: US$50/tonne for permanent CO2 storage
15% of energy consumption from renewables by 2030
14% renewable fuels target by 2030; 3.5% from advanced biofuels
US$13 billion between 2021 and 2030 to support technology scale-up
9.76% Renewable Transport Fuel Obligation by 2030
$1.8 billion to support Longship CCS project
increase wind and solar generation capacity to 1,200 gigawatts by 2030
25% renewable-power targets by 2030
3% renewable-fuel-blending ratio
60% of energy from renewables by 2030
20% reduction in the carbon intensity of transportation fuels by 2030 from 2011
US$38 billion in support for CCUS and hydrogen
approximately US$220 million available to support growth in the hydrogen industry
drive at least US$38 billion of new investment over the decade
is expected to raise up to $13 billion (EUR11.5 billion) between 2021 and 2030
over $1 billion (GBP800 million) to support four CCUS hubs and clusters
the United States expanded its federal 45Q tax credit, which provides $50/tonne for CO2 stored permanently
Under some scenarios, hydrogen demand could more than triple by 2050 if costs come down and infrastructure is built out.
Under the IEA’s SDS, hydrogen demand could more than triple by mid-century.
In the IEA’s Net-Zero 2050 (NZE2050) scenario, oil and gas demand fall to the SDS 2040 levels by 2030.
The IEA’s STEPS predicts oil demand to recover to pre-crisis levels by 2023.
The STEPS shows global oil demand growth slowing to about 750,000 barrels per day through 2030.
Demand in Asia is expected to grow by 40 to 50 percent from 2019 to 2030.
The global average oil production carbon intensity is 46 kg CO2e/boe.
The global average gas production carbon intensity is 71 kg CO2e/boe.
Demand for high-value petrochemicals, used to produce plastics, resins, and fibers, is projected to rise by 25 percent between 2019 and 2030 in the STEPS.
Biofuels production is expected to increase by 25 percent from 2019 to 2024.
The global average refining carbon intensity is 33 kg CO2e/boe.
The Paris Agreement, of keeping global average temperatures well below 2° C above pre-industrial levels and putting the world on track to achieve net-zero emissions by 2070.
Carbon pricing reaching more than $140/tonne.
net-zero emissions by 2050
In 2030, oil and gas constitute approximately 50 percent of the primary energy mix in the NZE2050 scenario, compared with 66 percent in the SDS.
Oil demand in NZE2050 is nearly 25 percent below SDS levels in 2030, whereas gas demand is about 8 percent below SDS levels in 2030.
In 2020, we undertook a stress-test exercise for our operated assets with regard to the potential upper workability conditions to 2050.
Our asset portfolio is generally resilient to acute and chronic hazards under RCP8.5 through 2030.
Resilience to acute hazards beyond 2030 may require additional hardening for specific assets.
Based on a comparison of the IEA’s WEO 2018 data, we estimate that more than 75 percent of our production of both oil and gas is below the global average carbon intensity for each commodity.
We are working to develop a product output–based methodology and in the interim have set a 1 to 2 percent emissions intensity reduction target on a throughput basis from 2016 to 2023.
we have set metrics for 2023 and 2028 and intend to do so every five years thereafter.
extending upstream production carbon-intensity metrics to 2028 oil 24 kg CO2e/boe gas 24 kg CO2e/boe flaring 3 kg CO2e/boe methane 2 kg CO2e/boe
Targeting a 40 percent reduction in oil carbon intensity
Targeting a 66 percent reduction in flaring carbon intensity
Targeting a 26 percent reduction in gas carbon intensity
Targeting a 53 percent reduction in methane intensity
we sourced opportunities from assets that represent approximately 70 percent of our equity GHG emissions.
We selected more than 60 projects to advance to execution, and plan to spend more than $100 million in 2021.
We expect to spend approximately $2 billion through 2028, on the path to deliver our 2028 performance metrics.
we have additional MACC opportunities identified that have the potential to lower our Upstream carbon intensity into the mid-teens.
Emissions associated with our own energy use make up about 70 percent of our Scope 1 and Scope 2 emissions.
Pavilion Energy Trading and Chevron have signed a five-year LNG sale and purchase agreement under which each LNG cargo delivered will be accompanied by a statement of its GHG emissions.

Within the Pathfinder, Chevron is working together with other committed stakeholders from across the value chain to develop a GHG quantification and reporting methodology for LNG based on internationally recognized standards.
Methane accounts for approximately 5 percent of our CO2e emissions.
We have installed more than 1,000 lower-emitting pneumatic controllers at these facilities since 2013.
We are actively addressing the reduction venting from the process unit by as much as 70 percent.
We have developed internal country-specific plans to minimize methane emissions representing 80 percent of our equity methane emissions.
Since 2013, we have reduced flaring and associated methane emissions.

Chevron is a signatory of the World Bank’s Zero Routine Flaring Initiative, which brings together governments, oil companies, and development institutions that agree to cooperate to eliminate routine flaring by no later than 2030.
OGCI member companies, including Chevron, have a methane-intensity target to reduce collective average upstream methane intensity to 0.20 per cent as a share of marketed gas, by 2025.
As of October 2020, member companies’ collective methane intensity was 0.23 percent.
65 megawatt wind-power purchase agreement in the Permian Basin, we are switching to a lower-carbon fuel source and working toward optimizing between purchased and self-generated power.
Chevron is partnering with Algonquin Power & Utilities Corporation to co-develop renewable-power projects that provide electricity to strategic assets across our global portfolio.

Chevron’s investments in battery technology include Natron Energy, which is developing a new generation of sodium-ion battery products that offer potential performance to integrate renewable power in support of our operations.
El Segundo supplies more than 20 percent of all motor vehicle fuels consumed in Southern California and is expected to start supplying the world’s growing energy needs.
In 2020, we announced the first renewable natural-gas production from dairy farms in the California Central Valley.
deep-sea zero-emissions vessels into operation by 2030 to support the International Maritime Organization’s ambition to reduce GHG emissions from shipping by at least 50 percent by 2050.
Chevron is piloting a new program in California to have five trees planted via the One Tree Planted organization every time a customer fills up at the pump.
We have committed $100 million to our Future Energy Fund, $300 million to our Future Energy Fund II, and $100 million to the OGCI Climate Investments fund.
This includes supporting total low-carbon funds commitment to $500 million, along with the more than $300 million invested in our Core Venture Fund.
Project investments were primarily in Canada and Australia and include the Gorgon CO2 injection project, one of the world’s largest integrated CCUS projects.
These projects are expected to reduce GHG emissions by nearly CCUS in key geographies.

We have invested more than $1 billion in CCUS research, development, and deployment opportunities to reduce our GHG emissions by 5 million tonnes per year, approximately equivalent to the GHG emissions from the average annual electricity usage in 660,000 U.S. homes.
Kern River Carbon Capture Project: Chevron was awarded a project from the U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) to pilot technology that captures CO2 from post-combustion gas.

Chevron is collaborating with Schlumberger New Energy, Microsoft, and Clean Energy Systems (CES), to work toward developing a bioenergy with carbon capture and sequestration (BECCS) project in Mendota, California.

The project is expected to result in net-negative emissions when fully operational, storing 300,000 tonnes of CO2 annually—equivalent to the emissions from the annual electricity usage of more than 65,000 U.S. homes.

Chevron is a member of the Oil and Gas Climate Initiative (OGCI), which is committed to industry-leading methane performance with a collective upstream methane intensity target below 0.25 percent, with the ambition to achieve 0.2 percent by 2025.

The Environmental Partnership, a voluntary industry effort to cut U.S. methane emissions that has conducted 184,000 leak-detection surveys and replaced more than 13,000 pneumatic controllers with low-/non-emitting technology.

Chevron partners with CalBio and Brightmark to produce and market renewable natural gas, helping reduce agricultural methane emissions while providing lower-carbon fuels, on a life-cycle basis, to our customers.
We continue to take action to further reduce methane emissions and have set a metric to reduce methane intensity by 53 percent by 2028.

Our combined $400 million efforts with the U.S. Department of Energy and Svante, as well as Future Energy Funds invest in promising projects in CCUS, reducing emissions by nearly 5 million tonnes per year.
We are investing in renewable fuels, products, and power, including sourcing over 500 megawatts of renewable generation by 2025.

Chevron calculates emissions from third-party use of our products in alignment with methods in Category 11 of IPIECA’s Estimating Petroleum Industry Value Chain (Scope 3) Greenhouse Gas Emissions (2016).
